items.title: Hook strength
items.description: High-scoring bakery copy makes the ordering moment visible within the first two lines: rushed breakfast, office sharing, afternoon tea, birthday planning, gift preparation, or weekend stock-up. Low-scoring copy opens with generic fragrance praise or product adjectives with no scene.
examples: High signal: the first line includes a concrete occasion, timing pressure, or use case.
High signal: the user can immediately tell why bakery is relevant right now.
Low signal: the copy starts with vague delicious or handmade language and no trigger moment.

items.title: Menu guidance quality
items.description: High-scoring copy helps users choose between breakfast breads, pastry desserts, whole cakes, sliced cakes, gift boxes, and sharing combinations. Low-scoring copy sounds tempting but never makes the order decision easier.
examples: High signal: the copy identifies one or two safe first-order routes.
High signal: single-person, family, celebration, and group-sharing needs are separated clearly.
Low signal: the product list grows longer but the path to ordering stays unclear.

items.title: Scene, audience, and channel fit
items.description: High-scoring bakery copy maps store advantages to real contexts such as commuting, school-drop breakfast, office pastry sharing, birthday pickup, home delivery boundaries, or holiday gifting. Low-scoring copy uses one flat message for every customer and every order route.
examples: High signal: breakfast copy discusses portability, timing, and quick choice.
High signal: cake copy discusses headcount, style fit, and pickup planning.
Low signal: the same broad pitch is used for bread, cake, and gift-box scenarios.

items.title: Texture-to-decision translation
items.description: High-scoring copy converts softness, crispness, butter aroma, cream lightness, fruit freshness, and filling density into practical reasons to buy, carry, share, or preorder. Low-scoring copy stops at sensory praise and never explains why the product fits the user's decision.
examples: High signal: soft bread is translated into easier breakfast use or kid acceptance.
High signal: lighter cream is translated into celebration suitability across age groups.
Low signal: layered, rich, and fragrant appear without any order meaning.

items.title: Trust and freshness evidence density
items.description: High-scoring bakery copy uses real proof such as batch timing, product sizing, writing and candle options, packaging logic, pickup flow, cut-size guidance, and local convenience. Low-scoring copy leans on hype, empty popularity claims, or unverifiable freshness absolutes.
examples: High signal: process details support the sales angle.
High signal: freshness is explained through rhythm, not slogans.
Low signal: the copy relies on everyone buys this or always just baked language.

items.title: Offer and customization clarity
items.description: High-scoring copy explains size, weight, slice count, writing service, candles, decoration scope, flavor upgrades, gift packaging, and pricing boundaries. Low-scoring copy implies that all cakes, boxes, or add-ons fit one exact quote.
examples: High signal: the user can tell what changes the final price.
High signal: basic styles and more complex customization routes are clearly separated.
Low signal: the copy uses one-price language without conditions.

items.title: Local fulfillment completeness
items.description: High-scoring copy reduces logistical friction by clarifying store relevance, pickup windows, delivery boundaries, cold-chain sensitivity, peak-hour expectations, and preorder timing. Low-scoring copy ignores the actual last-mile step between desire and receiving the bakery item.
examples: High signal: the copy tells the user when and how to pick up more smoothly.
High signal: refrigerated cake handling is described conservatively.
Low signal: no pickup, delivery, or timing information appears at all.

items.title: Emotional resonance
items.description: High-scoring copy captures the real emotional need behind the purchase: avoiding a chaotic morning, showing up with a decent birthday cake, bringing something appropriate to the office, or taking home a better weekend breakfast rhythm. Low-scoring copy only describes products and never the human reason for buying them.
examples: High signal: the user can see their own routine or celebration pressure in the copy.
High signal: the order feels tied to relief, care, or occasion-fit rather than just appetite.
Low signal: the brand talks only from the display-case perspective.

items.title: Risk control
items.description: High-scoring copy avoids health-function claims, body claims, diabetic-safe implications, absolute freshness promises, unsupported ranking language, guaranteed custom speed, and guaranteed intact delivery promises. Low-scoring copy overreaches on wellness, safety, popularity, price, or fulfillment certainty.
examples: High signal: risky expressions are replaced with portion, sweetness, and scene-based language.
High signal: time and delivery expectations are framed conservatively.
Low signal: the copy promises not fattening desserts, citywide best taste, or one-hour custom cakes.

items.title: CTA clarity
items.description: High-scoring CTA asks for useful next-step information such as date, headcount, pickup time, flavor direction, budget, writing needs, candles, gift intent, and delivery versus pickup route. Low-scoring CTA only says message us or come order without shaping the next move.
examples: High signal: the CTA tells the user exactly what to send back.
High signal: breakfast, cake, and group-sharing CTAs ask for different inputs.
Low signal: the CTA stays generic and does not collect decision variables.

items.title: Tone consistency
items.description: High-scoring copy keeps the same grounded tone across hook, body, and CTA. Low-scoring copy starts with warm lifestyle language and then suddenly shifts into fake scarcity, hard-selling urgency, or unsupported promises, which breaks trust.
examples: High signal: the tone stays calm, useful, and occasion-aware throughout.
High signal: persuasion comes from fit and proof rather than pressure spikes.
Low signal: the ending jumps into panic language that the opening did not prepare.
